g.data-narration: Welcome to Binary Adder-Subtractor. This fundamental circuit forms the heart of every computer's arithmetic logic unit.
Adders are the foundation of CPU arithmetic. Every math operation your computer performs uses these circuits at the hardware level.
g.text: Addition and subtraction are the core operations in every
CPU. The ALU uses these circuits for ALL mathematical
operations - from simple counters to complex calculations.

g.data-narration: We will learn to build half adders, full adders, multi-bit adders, and the combined adder-subtractor circuit with overflow detection.
g.text: Half Adder: 2-bit addition with Sum and Carry
Full Adder: Adding with carry-in from previous stage

g.data-narration: A half adder adds two single binary bits. It produces a Sum (like digit addition) and a Carry (when 1+1 equals 10 in binary).
g.text: Takes two inputs: A and B (each 0 or 1)

g.data-narration: The truth table shows all four possible inputs and their outputs. Notice how Carry is only 1 when both inputs are 1.
The half adder circuit uses an XOR gate for sum and an AND gate for carry. These are the two fundamental building blocks.
A full adder adds THREE bits: A, B, and a carry-in from the previous stage. This allows us to chain adders together.
g.text: Takes three inputs: A, B, and Carry-in (Cᵢₙ)
Essential for cascading adders in multi-bit operations

g.data-narration: Eight possible input combinations exist. Notice the pattern: carry-out is 1 when at least two inputs are 1.
A full adder can be built using two half adders and an OR gate. First half adder adds A and B, second adds result to Cᵢₙ.
A 4-bit ripple carry adder chains four full adders. The carry
g.text: Four full adders cascaded, carry propagates from FA₀ → FA₃

g.data-narration: Subtraction uses 2's complement: A - B equals A + (B's complement + 1). We invert B and add 1 through carry-in.
g.text: A - B = A + (2's complement of B) = A + B' + 1
B' = bit-wise inversion (1's complement), adding 1 via carry-in

g.data-narration: A unified circuit uses XOR gates and a mode bit M. M=0 adds normally. M=1 inverts B and sets carry-in to 1 for subtraction.
g.text: Mode M controls operation: M=0 → Add (A+B), M=1 → Subtract (A-B)

g.data-narration: Overflow occurs in signed arithmetic when result exceeds range. Detected by XORing the two MSB carries: V = Cₙ ⊕ Cₙ₋₁.
g.text: V = Cₙ ⊕ Cₙ₋₁ (XOR of MSB carry-out and carry-in)
If V=1: overflow occurred (wrong sign in signed arithmetic)
Overflow! Two positives gave negative result
